content:
You are an LLVM compiler expert specializing in test case generation and bug detection. You are conducting LLVM code review now.

## Context
Maintainers provided a patch, but patches often have edge cases or introduce new issues.

## Your Task
You will work in two phases:
- Phase 1: Analyze the fix to identify potential issues, gaps, and edge cases. Propose test strategies.
- Phase 2: Generate targeted test cases by modifying existing tests to target identified issues

## You Will Receive
- Original patch description and related test case(s)
- The code region that was modified (the patch)
- Information about the optimization pass and component involved

## Tools You Can Use

You have multiple tools for each phase, but you should try to avoid using them with same inputs repeatedly to reduce redundant calls.

**CRITICAL**: You MUST use tool call every action in Phase 1 and Phase 2.

# Phase 1: Analyze the Fix

This is a patch for SLPVectorizer:

[SLP] Fix logic for commuting copyable operands in buildInstructionsState()
Noticed by inspection, if my understanding is correct the existing logic is incorrect.

for the following:
%add = add i32 %0, %1
%add = add i32 3, %3
%add = add i32 %4, %5
%urem = urem i32 %6, %7

Operands would contain (since `urem`  will be `Copyable`):
```
Operands[0] = {%0, 3, %4, %6}
Operands[1] = {%1, %3, %5, %7}
```
With the existing logic, we would first compare `%0` and `%6`, and then compare `%1` and `%7`. What I believe is intended is to compare `%0` and `%1`, then `3` and `%3`, and so on.

diff --git a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/SLPVectorizer.cpp b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/SLPVectorizer.cpp
index 4caa1707f0f27..714c7456ce10c 100644
--- a/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/SLPVectorizer.cpp
+++ b/llvm/lib/Transforms/Vectorize/SLPVectorizer.cpp
@@ -11621,17 +11621,11 @@ class InstructionsCompatibilityAnalysis {
     // Check profitability if number of copyables > VL.size() / 2.
     // 1. Reorder operands for better matching.
     if (isCommutative(MainOp)) {
-      for (auto &Ops : Operands) {
-        // Make instructions the first operands.
-        if (!isa<Instruction>(Ops.front()) && isa<Instruction>(Ops.back())) {
-          std::swap(Ops.front(), Ops.back());
-          continue;
-        }
-        // Make constants the second operands.
-        if (isa<Constant>(Ops.front())) {
-          std::swap(Ops.front(), Ops.back());
-          continue;
-        }
+      for (auto [OpA, OpB] : zip(Operands.front(), Operands.back())) {
+        // Make instructions the first operand and constants the second.
+        if ((!isa<Instruction>(OpA) && isa<Instruction>(OpB)) ||
+            isa<Constant>(OpA))
+          std::swap(OpA, OpB);
       }
     }
     // 2. Check, if operands can be vectorized.
diff --git a/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/bv-root-part-of-graph.ll b/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/bv-root-part-of-graph.ll
index 1abc16da77c8e..0cc4d3db5c537 100644
--- a/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/bv-root-part-of-graph.ll
+++ b/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/bv-root-part-of-graph.ll
@@ -4,16 +4,15 @@
 define void @test() {
 ; CHECK-LABEL: define void @test() {
 ; CHECK-NEXT:  [[BB:.*]]:
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP0:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x float> <float 0.000000e+00, float undef, float 0.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00>, <4 x float> poison, <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 0, i32 2, i32 3>
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP1:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x float> <float 0.000000e+00, float undef, float 0.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00>, <4 x float> <float poison, float 0.000000e+00, float poison, float poison>, <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 5, i32 2, i32 3>
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    br label %[[BB1:.*]]
 ; CHECK:       [[BB1]]:
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[PHI:%.*]] = phi i32 [ 0, %[[BB]] ], [ [[TMP9:%.*]], %[[BB1]] ]
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[FMUL:%.*]] = sitofp i32 0 to float
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[SITOFP:%.*]] = sitofp i32 0 to float
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP0:%.*]] = insertelement <4 x float> <float poison, float 1.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00>, float [[SITOFP]], i32 0
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP3:%.*]] = fmul <4 x float> <float 1.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00>, [[TMP0]]
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP1:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x float> [[TMP0]], <4 x float> <float poison, float poison, float poison, float 0.000000e+00>, <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 0, i32 poison, i32 7>
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[FMUL:%.*]] = fmul float 0.000000e+00, 0.000000e+00
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP2:%.*]] = insertelement <4 x float> [[TMP1]], float [[FMUL]], i32 2
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP4:%.*]] = fadd <4 x float> [[TMP2]], [[TMP3]]
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP3:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x float> [[TMP2]], <4 x float> poison, <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 1, i32 2, i32 2>
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP4:%.*]] = fadd <4 x float> [[TMP0]], [[TMP3]]
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP5:%.*]] = fadd <4 x float> [[TMP4]], zeroinitializer
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP6:%.*]] = fcmp ogt <4 x float> [[TMP5]], zeroinitializer
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP7:%.*]] = select <4 x i1> [[TMP6]], <4 x i32> zeroinitializer, <4 x i32> zeroinitializer
diff --git a/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/copyable-with-used-outside-bb.ll b/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/copyable-with-used-outside-bb.ll
index 7f13b30e35b21..c19aa07229f2a 100644
--- a/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/copyable-with-used-outside-bb.ll
+++ b/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/copyable-with-used-outside-bb.ll
@@ -9,14 +9,15 @@ define void @test(i32 %arg) {
 ; CHECK:       [[BB1]]:
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP0:%.*]] = insertelement <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 0, i32 poison, i32 0>, i32 [[ARG]], i32 2
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP1:%.*]] = sub <4 x i32> <i32 7, i32 7, i32 0, i32 7>, [[TMP0]]
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP4:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x i32> [[TMP1]], <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 0, i32 poison, i32 0>, <4 x i32> <i32 4, i32 5, i32 2, i32 7>
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP3:%.*]] = add <4 x i32> <i32 7, i32 0, i32 0, i32 0>, [[TMP4]]
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP2:%.*]] = extractelement <4 x i32> [[TMP1]], i32 0
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[ICMP:%.*]] = icmp ult i32 [[TMP2]], 0
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP3:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x i32> [[TMP1]], <4 x i32> <i32 poison, i32 0, i32 poison, i32 0>, <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 5, i32 2, i32 7>
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    br i1 false, label %[[BB2:.*]], label %[[BB3:.*]]
 ; CHECK:       [[BB2]]:
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    br label %[[BB3]]
 ; CHECK:       [[BB3]]:
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP4:%.*]] = phi <4 x i32> [ [[TMP1]], %[[BB2]] ], [ [[TMP3]], %[[BB1]] ]
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP5:%.*]] = phi <4 x i32> [ [[TMP1]], %[[BB2]] ], [ [[TMP3]], %[[BB1]] ]
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    ret void
 ;
 bb:
diff --git a/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/non-schedulable-node-with-non-schedulable-parent.ll b/llvm/test/Transforms/SLPVectorizer/X86/non-schedulable-node-with-non-schedulable-parent.ll
index 34d9944b5375f..cf6ff878ef966 100644

 define float @test() {
 ; CHECK-LABEL: define float @test() {
 ; CHECK-NEXT:  [[BB:.*:]]
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[FADD:%.*]] = fadd float 0.000000e+00, 0.000000e+00
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[LOAD:%.*]] = load float, ptr null, align 4
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[FADD2:%.*]] = fadd float 0.000000e+00, 0.000000e+00
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP2:%.*]] = insertelement <4 x float> <float poison, float poison, float 0.000000e+00, float poison>, float [[FADD]], i32 3
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP1:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x float> [[TMP2]], <4 x float> <float 0.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00, float undef, float undef>, <4 x i32> <i32 4, i32 5, i32 2, i32 3>
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP0:%.*]] = insertelement <4 x float> <float 0.000000e+00, float -0.000000e+00, float -0.000000e+00, float poison>, float [[LOAD]], i32 3
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP1:%.*]] = fadd <4 x float> zeroinitializer, [[TMP0]]
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP3:%.*]] = fadd <4 x float> [[TMP1]], [[TMP0]]
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    br i1 false, label %[[BB5:.*]], label %[[BB6:.*]]
 ; CHECK:       [[BB5]]:
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    br label %[[BB8:.*]]
 ; CHECK:       [[BB6]]:
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP4:%.*]] = insertelement <2 x float> poison, float [[FADD2]], i32 0
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP11:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x float> [[TMP3]], <4 x float> poison, <2 x i32> <i32 poison, i32 3>
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP6:%.*]] = shufflevector <2 x float> [[TMP4]], <2 x float> [[TMP11]], <2 x i32> <i32 0, i32 3>
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP5:%.*]] = insertelement <4 x float> <float poison, float 0.000000e+00, float poison, float poison>, float [[LOAD]], i32 0
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP3:%.*]] = insertelement <4 x float> [[TMP5]], float [[FADD2]], i32 2
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP7:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x float> [[TMP3]], <4 x float> [[TMP1]], <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 1, i32 2, i32 7>
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP12:%.*]] = shufflevector <2 x float> [[TMP6]], <2 x float> poison, <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 1, i32 poison, i32 poison>
+; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP7:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x float> [[TMP5]], <4 x float> [[TMP12]], <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 1, i32 4, i32 5>
 ; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP8:%.*]] = fdiv <4 x float> [[TMP7]], <float 1.000000e+00, float 1.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00, float 0.000000e+00>

 ; CHECK:       [[BB8]]:
-; CHECK-NEXT:    [[TMP9:%.*]] = phi <4 x float> [ [[TMP1]], %[[BB5]] ], [ [[TMP8]], %[[BB6]] ]

+; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP11:%.*]] = shufflevector <8 x double> [[TMP7]], <8 x double> poison, <4 x i32> <i32 2, i32 0, i32 poison, i32 poison>
 ; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP12:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x double> [[TMP11]], <4 x double> <double poison, double poison, double poison, double 0.000000e+00>, <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 1, i32 poison, i32 7>
 ; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP13:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x double> [[TMP12]], <4 x double> [[TMP4]], <4 x i32> <i32 0, i32 1, i32 5, i32 3>
-; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP14:%.*]] = fmul <4 x double> [[TMP6]], [[TMP13]]
+; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP14:%.*]] = fmul <4 x double> [[TMP10]], [[TMP13]]
 ; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP15:%.*]] = shufflevector <4 x double> [[TMP14]], <4 x double> poison, <8 x i32> <i32 0, i32 1, i32 2, i32 3, i32 poison, i32 poison, i32 poison, i32 poison>
 ; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP16:%.*]] = shufflevector <8 x double> <double poison, double poison, double poison, double poison, double 0.000000e+00, double 0.000000e+00, double 0.000000e+00, double 0.000000e+00>, <8 x double> [[TMP15]], <8 x i32> <i32 8, i32 9, i32 10, i32 11, i32 4, i32 5, i32 6, i32 7>
-; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP17:%.*]] = fsub <8 x double> [[TMP16]], [[TMP10]]
-; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP18:%.*]] = fmul <8 x double> [[TMP16]], [[TMP10]]
+; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP17:%.*]] = fsub <8 x double> [[TMP16]], [[TMP8]]
+; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP18:%.*]] = fmul <8 x double> [[TMP16]], [[TMP8]]
 ; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP19:%.*]] = shufflevector <8 x double> [[TMP17]], <8 x double> [[TMP18]], <8 x i32> <i32 0, i32 1, i32 2, i32 3, i32 4, i32 13, i32 14, i32 15>
 ; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP20:%.*]] = fptrunc <8 x double> [[TMP19]] to <8 x float>
 ; AARCH64-NEXT:    [[TMP21:%.*]] = fmul <8 x float> [[TMP20]], zeroinitializer


---

## Subsystem Knowledge for SLPVectorizer

For reference, here are some key points about the SLPVectorizer you should consider (but do not limit to these) in your analysis:

# Subsystem Knowledge for SLPVectorizer

## Elements Frequently Missed

*   **Short-Circuiting and Poison-Masking Semantics**: The vectorizer frequently misses the implicit poison-masking behavior of scalar short-circuiting instructions (like `select`, logical AND/OR). When converting these to eager vector operations or reordering them, it fails to realize that unevaluated `poison` values in the scalar code will become evaluated and propagate in the vectorized code.
*   **Multi-Use and Shared Node Constraints**: When a scalar value or intermediate tree node is used in multiple different contexts, the vectorizer often only checks the constraints of one context. It misses the strict requirements of other external uses, leading to unsafe type demotions or truncations.
*   **Strict Poison vs. Undef Differentiation**: The optimization pass frequently treats `undef` and `poison` interchangeably or fails to check if a reused vector lane contains `poison`. It misses the fact that replacing `undef` with `poison` (or reusing a mask with `poison` lanes for a fully defined splat) violates IR semantics by making the program more poisonous.
*   **Instruction Flags on Mutated Operations**: Fast-math or optimization flags (such as `samesign` on `icmp`) are frequently missed or incorrectly retained when the underlying operands are mutated (e.g., truncated). The pass forgets to drop these flags when the transformation invalidates the assumptions they represent.
*   **Signedness Information Across Opaque Instructions**: The vectorizer frequently loses track of individual scalar signedness requirements (whether to `sext` or `zext`) when scalars are grouped into a uniform vector node, or when the values pass through instructions like `freeze` that obscure the original extension semantics.
*   **Dynamic/Non-Constant Indices**: The pass misses the conservative tracking required for `insertelement` operations with dynamic (non-constant) indices. Instead of assuming the insertion could affect any lane, it ignores the dynamic index, leading to incorrect element tracking and dropped instructions.
*   **64-bit Pointer Offsets**: During memory access analysis, the vectorizer misses the upper 32 bits of 64-bit pointer offsets. By truncating pointer differences to 32 bits, it incorrectly identifies widely separated, non-contiguous memory accesses as contiguous.
*   **Bitwidth-Sensitive Operands**: When demoting the bitwidth of alternate opcode sequences, the pass misses the specific operand constraints of bitwidth-sensitive instructions (like `shl`). It applies the demotion uniformly, causing shift amounts to exceed the new, narrower bitwidth.
*   **Cost-Model Artifacts**: The pass occasionally misses the distinction between temporary IR constructs (like subregister masks generated purely for cost estimation) and actual executable IR, erroneously leaking the cost-model masks into the final `shufflevector` instructions.

## Patterns Not Well Handled

### Pattern 1: Bitwidth Reduction and Type Demotion
The SLP vectorizer struggles significantly with safely reducing the bitwidth of vectorized integer operations. The core issue is that the pass applies demotion too aggressively without maintaining a global view of all constraints. It fails to handle shared nodes with conflicting strict/permissive contexts, incorrectly applies "ignore lists" to intermediate tree nodes instead of just roots, and loses track of mixed signedness requirements when extracting narrowed scalars for external uses. Furthermore, it blindly applies demotion to alternate opcode sequences without respecting bitwidth-sensitive operations (like shifts), and forgets to strip optimization flags (like `samesign`) that become invalid in the narrower type.

### Pattern 2: Shuffle Mask Generation for Gathers and Mixed-Size Vectors
Generating correct `shufflevector` masks when combining multiple source vectors into a single gather or build-vector operation is a highly error-prone pattern. The vectorizer frequently miscalculates index offsets when the source vectors have different lengths (often basing the offset solely on the first vector's length or the resulting mask's size). It also struggles with calculating the correct slice sizes when the number of gathered scalars diverges from the original tree entry count, and fails to correctly compose masks when gathered scalars are reused across multiple lanes. This results in malformed shuffles that extract the wrong elements or pad the vector with poison.

### Pattern 3: Vectorization of Short-Circuiting Boolean Reductions
The pass does not well handle the conversion of scalar boolean reduction chains (e.g., `select` chains representing logical AND/OR) into horizontal vector reductions. Scalar chains inherently short-circuit, safely masking `poison` or `undef` values if an earlier condition dictates the result. Vector reductions, however, evaluate all lanes eagerly. The vectorizer fails to insert necessary `freeze` instructions or preserve the evaluation order, causing `poison` values from out-of-bounds extractions or undefined lanes to propagate into the final reduction result, fundamentally breaking the original program semantics.

### Pattern 4: Unification of Alternate Opcodes and Identity Operations
When the vectorizer encounters isomorphic chains with different opcodes (e.g., `xor X, 0` and `mul X, 1`), it attempts to unify them into a single vector instruction by treating identity operations as universally interchangeable. This pattern is poorly handled because the pass assumes it can simply pick one opcode and blend the constants. It fails to realize that there is no valid semantic translation between fundamentally different operations (bitwise vs. arithmetic). Consequently, it generates flawed constant conversions (e.g., `mul X, 0`), destroying the identity semantics and zeroing out valid data.

### Pattern 5: Vector and Instruction Reuse with Poison/Undef
To optimize code generation, the vectorizer attempts to reuse existing vectors or `shufflevector` instructions (such as splats or gathers) that match the required operands. However, the matching logic is flawed because it does not strictly verify the compatibility of `poison` and `undef` lanes. It will replace an `undef` operand with a reused vector lane containing `poison`, or reuse a splat mask containing `poison` lanes for an operation that strictly requires valid data. This pattern makes the resulting IR more poisonous than the original scalar code.

### Pattern 6: Memory Access Analysis and Reordering
The vectorizer mishandles the analysis and transformation of strided or non-contiguous memory loads. When scalar loads require reordering to match the vector lane semantics, the pass sometimes generates widened, contiguous vector loads without emitting the necessary compensating `shufflevector` masks, leading to incorrect lane assignments and potentially unsafe out-of-bounds memory accesses. Additionally, its pointer distance analysis is flawed for 64-bit architectures, as it truncates offsets to 32 bits, erroneously grouping distant memory accesses into single contiguous vector operations.

Do NOT start your analysis from these patterns. Start from the patch semantics. Use these **ONLY** to cross-check your reasoning.

Based on the model built for this fix, you should segment the fix into smaller parts and analyze the semantics of each part. Follow this structured workflow for segmentation and analysis:

- **Segmentation**: Break down the fix into smaller segments or key changes, especially focusing on code regions related to semantics changes.
- **Analysis**: For each segment, analyze the semantics and identify any assumptions or preconditions that the fix relies on.

#### Tips for this step

{"strategies":[{"expected_issue":"Missed vectorization: SLP refuses to vectorize because Operands[1] still contains multiple Instruction operands (count_if(Operands.back(), Instruction)>1), even though commuting per-lane would reduce that count; CHECK will show scalar ops remain instead of a vector add/or/and.","name":"Per-lane commutation must enable vectorization (instruction/constant mixed and reversed per lane)","rationale":"The new code intends to commute operands *per lane* between operand-lists (Operands[0][i] <-> Operands[1][i]) so that instructions end up in the first operand list and constants in the second. However, `for (auto [OpA, OpB] : zip(...))` with `auto` structured bindings drops references, so `std::swap(OpA, OpB)` may not mutate `Operands` at all. A test that only becomes legal/profitable with correct per-lane commuting will catch this (it will fail to vectorize if the swap is ineffective).","target":"Mutate/add an SLP test with a commutative opcode (e.g. add/or/xor) where across lanes operand order alternates: lane0 has (inst, cst), lane1 has (cst, inst), lane2 has (inst, cst), lane3 has (cst, inst). Ensure >1 instruction ends up in Operands.back() unless swaps happen. Use -passes=slp-vectorizer and FileCheck for a single vector binop."},{"expected_issue":"Regression in profitability/legality: buildInstructionsState returns invalid when it should succeed, or succeeds when it should be invalid, depending on whether operand reordering is applied correctly. Observable as changed presence/absence of a vector binop or as different root chosen.","name":"Copyable-element mixture where wrong commutation breaks matching (copyable non-Instruction values + real instructions)","rationale":"Copyable elements are represented as `{V, identity}`; if V is an argument/phi/non-instruction (copyable) and the other operand is an instruction, the commuting heuristic should put the instruction operand in Operands[0] and the identity constant in Operands[1]. Without the intended swap, Operands[1] may look non-vectorizable or contain multiple instructions, causing early invalidation. This stresses the exact scenario described in the patch motivation (non-uniform mix of copyables and real ops).","target":"Mutate an existing test to include a bundle of adds where some lanes are real adds, some lanes are copyable values like `%arg`/`%phi`/`undef` (but not poison), and some lanes include a different-opcode instruction (e.g. `urem`) that becomes copyable. Alternate operand order per lane. Check that vectorization still happens and does not fall back to scalar."},{"expected_issue":"Nondeterministic / incorrect operand classification when constants are ConstantExprs or splats: reordering may treat ConstantExpr as Constant and swap unexpectedly (or fail to), changing whether operand is detected as splat/almost-splat. Observable as either missed vectorization or different shufflevector construction.","name":"ConstantExpr/GlobalAddress constants in commuted positions","rationale":"The heuristic uses `isa<Constant>(OpA)` to decide swapping. ConstantExpr, GlobalValue, and constant vectors all satisfy Constant. If per-lane commuting works, it will push these into operand1. This can flip `allConstant/isSplat/almost-splat` decisions in CheckOperand and thus profitability. If swapping is ineffective, behavior differs. Also stresses that constant-ness is broader than literal integers/floats.","target":"Create lanes where one operand is `ptrtoint (ptr @g to i64)` or `add (i32 ptrtoint...)` as ConstantExpr, and the other is an instruction. Alternate operand order. Verify vectorization legality and stable resulting vector binop."},{"expected_issue":"Incorrectly accepted vectorization (or crash) due to zip-shortest: if operand lists ever diverge in size unexpectedly, zip() silently ignores the tail. This could leave some lanes uncommuted and later logic inconsistent. Expected symptom: assertion failure in debug, or miscompile/missed vectorization in release.","name":"Stress operand-list size invariants around copyable lowering","rationale":"The new loop uses `zip(Operands.front(), Operands.back())`, which iterates to the shortest range. The code asserts `Operands.size()==2` but not that both ValueLists are same length (they should be VL.size()). Any bug elsewhere that produces mismatched lengths would now be silently masked, whereas the old code iterated each list independently. A targeted test can try to create situations where operand construction might insert poison placeholders or skip operands (e.g., mixing non-instructions/poison in VL) and then verify behavior doesn’t change or crash. This is a guard against new silent truncation behavior.","target":"Mutate an existing test to include PoisonValue lanes in VL (e.g., via `select i1 %c, i32 %x, i32 poison` feeding the bundle) so buildOperands may contain poison placeholders. Ensure commutation is still applied consistently and no crash; check vectorization result is deterministic."},{"expected_issue":"Semantic/perf regression: vectorization should be enabled (or disabled) consistently when only one side is vectorizable and the other is 'almost splat'. If commutation is wrong/ineffective, CheckOperand is applied to the wrong side and may incorrectly reject.","name":"Almost-splat vs. vectorizable operand side selection","rationale":"After reordering, the code calls `CheckOperand(Operands.front())` and additionally requires `Operands.back()` to have <=1 instruction. The intention is to make `Operands.front()` the easier-to-vectorize side (constants/splat/almost-splat). If swapping is ineffective, the sides may be reversed, causing `CheckOperand` to run on the harder side and reject. This is a pure analysis/profitability bug that will show up as missing vectorization.","target":"Construct a commutative bundle where one operand across lanes is 'almost splat' (>=4 lanes, only one differing non-undef value) and the other operand is a vectorizable instruction chain; alternate operand order per lane. Check that vectorization occurs and uses the almost-splat side as the constant/splat operand in the vector binop."}],"thoughts":"### 1. Fix Understanding
`InstructionsCompatibilityAnalysis::buildInstructionsState()` has a profitability sub-check for the special mode “vectorize copyable elements”. It builds `Operands` (two operand-lists for a binary op) and then, for commutative ops, tries to reorder operands to improve matching/profitability: put instruction-like operands on operand 0 and constants on operand 1.

**Before patch**: it iterated `for (auto &Ops : Operands)` and swapped `Ops.front()`/`Ops.back()` inside each operand list. That swaps *lanes* within the same operand, not the two operands of the instruction, so it compares/matches the wrong things (as described in the patch text).

**After patch**: it intends to iterate lane-wise across the two operand lists and commute the per-lane operands by swapping `Operands[0][i]` and `Operands[1][i]` when beneficial.

### 2. Formal Semantic Modeling
Let `VL = [I0, I1, ..., I(n-1)]` be the bundle. For a commutative binary op, each scalar instruction has operands `(a_i, b_i)`. The analysis creates two operand lists:
- `Operands[0] = [a_0, a_1, ..., a_{n-1}]`
- `Operands[1] = [b_0, b_1, ..., b_{n-1}]`

**Desired commuting contract** (analysis-only): for each lane i, optionally swap `(a_i, b_i)` (i.e. commute the operation in lane i) to make `Operands[0][i]` more “instruction-like” and `Operands[1][i]` more “constant-like”, improving the subsequent checks:
- reject if `Operands[1]` contains > 1 instruction
- accept if `Operands[0]` is all-constant / splat / almost-splat / or recursively vectorizable

**Old behavior** effectively did:
- for each operand list j in {0,1}: maybe swap `Operands[j][0]` with `Operands[j][n-1]`.
This is unrelated to commuting and can break intended pairing. For example with n=4:
Operands[0]=[%0, 3, %4, %6], Operands[1]=[%1, %3, %5, %7].
Old logic may compare/match `%0` with `%6` etc., not `%0` with `%1`.

**New behavior intent** is per-lane swap:
for i=0..n-1:
if (needSwap(Operands[0][i], Operands[1][i])) swap(Operands[0][i], Operands[1][i]).

**Important risk**: in C++17 structured bindings, `auto [OpA, OpB] = tuple_of_refs;` typically drops references in deduction, so `OpA`/`OpB` become copies of `Value*` rather than `Value*&`. Then `std::swap(OpA, OpB)` does not modify `Operands`. In that case, the intended commuting never happens, so profitability/legality may regress.

Concrete instantiation for detecting the issue:
- Lane0: add %inst0, 1
- Lane1: add 2, %inst1
- Lane2: add %inst2, 3
- Lane3: add 4, %inst3
Without correct per-lane swap, `Operands[1]` contains `%inst1` and `%inst3` (2 instructions) and the analysis may reject; with correct swap, instructions are moved to `Operands[0

That includes ConstantExpr/GlobalValue. A per-lane commute should push such constants to operand1. If the swap does not mutate Operands, profitability/legality and the generated shuffles can change. This broadens coverage beyond literal ints/floats.","target":"Add lanes using ConstantExpr (e.g., ptrtoint @g, add constant) mixed with instruction operands, with alternating operand order. FileCheck for stable vectorization pattern and no scalar fallback."},{"expected_issue":"Potential silent truncation if operand list sizes ever diverge: zip() iterates to shortest; tail lanes would be unprocessed, leading to inconsistent state or miscompile/crash elsewhere.","name":"Stress invariants with poison/undef lanes in the bundle","rationale":"New loop uses zip(Operands.front(), Operands.back()) which stops at shortest. While both lists should be VL.size(), introducing poison/undef and non-instruction placeholders stresses operand construction paths. This is a safety net against future changes producing mismatched sizes that would now be silently ignored.","target":"Create a bundle containing some poison/undef-derived values (e.g., via select producing poison on some lanes) and ensure SLP still either vectorizes correctly or cleanly rejects without crashing; check for deterministic output."}],"thoughts":"1. Fix Understanding
The patch changes operand reordering in SLPVectorizer::InstructionsCompatibilityAnalysis::buildInstructionsState() when vectorizing commutative operations with many copyable elements. Previously it incorrectly swapped the first/last *lanes* within each operand list, rather than commuting operands *per lane*. The new code intends to iterate lane-wise across Operands[0] and Operands[1] and swap each pair to place instruction-like values in operand0 and constants in operand1, improving subsequent profitability/legality checks.

2. Formal Semantic Modeling
Given a commutative binary op with per-lane operands (a_i,b_i), analysis constructs Operands[0][i]=a_i and Operands[1][i]=b_i (or for copyables: {V, identity}). The intended transformation is: for each i, optionally swap Operands[0][i] and Operands[1][i] based on heuristics. Old code instead swapped Operands[j][0] <-> Operands[j][n-1], which is unrelated to commuting and can destroy pairing.
Concrete instantiation: four adds with alternating operand order (inst,cst)/(cst,inst). Without per-lane commute, Operands[1] can contain multiple instructions, causing rejection at `count_if(Operands.back(), Instruction)>1`. With correct commuting, instructions move to Operands[0], leaving <=1 instruction in Operands[1] and allowing vectorization.

3. Assumptions and Preconditions Identified
- Operands.size()==2 and both ValueLists have identical length (VL.size()).
- Structured binding in `for (auto [OpA, OpB] : zip(...))` actually provides references so swapping mutates Operands.
- Commuting based on `isa<Instruction>` and `isa<Constant>` is sufficient to improve matching without changing legality.

4. Potential Cases to Break Assumptions
- If structured bindings drop references, swapping does not modify Operands, making the fix ineffective (regression persists).
- Constants that are ConstantExpr/GlobalValue widen the scope of `isa<Constant>` and can cause unexpected swaps (or no swap if ineffective).
- Poison/undef and copyable lowering paths stress operand construction; if future changes ever cause mismatched operand list lengths, zip-shortest would silently skip tail lanes.

5. Test Strategies
See strategies list: focus on (a) patterns that only vectorize with correct per-lane commuting, (b) copyable-element mixtures matching the motivating example, (c) almost-splat vs vectorizable side sensitivity, (d) ConstantExpr constants, (e) poison/undef stress for invariants."}

You have proposed the following test strategies in Phase 1:

When VL contains a mix of main-op instructions and copyable different-opcode instructions (e.g., urem in an add bundle) plus constants, commuting is necessary to keep Operands[1] simple. This targets the motivating example.', 'expected_issue': 'Regression with copyable elements represented as {V, identity}: failure to commute leaves identity constants mixed with instructions on the wrong operand-list, causing rejection or different roots.'}, {'name': 'ConstantExpr and global-address constants as commuted operands', 'target': 'Add lanes using ConstantExpr (e.g., ptrtoint @g, add constant) mixed with instruction operands, with alternating operand order. While both lists should be VL.size(), introducing poison/undef and non-instruction placeholders stresses operand construction paths. This is a safety net against future changes producing mismatched sizes that would now be silently ignored.', 'expected_issue': 'Potential silent truncation if operand list sizes ever diverge: zip() iterates to shortest; tail lanes would be unprocessed, leading to inconsistent state or miscompile/crash elsewhere.'}]

## Your Task for Phase 2

In this phase, you will use the `tests_manager` tool to retrieve existing test cases, apply your proposed mutations, and verify them. You must ensure that **every test case** managed by the `tests_manager` is processed and marked as tested.

Follow this structured workflow:

### Step 1: Retrieve and Prioritize Test Cases
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `list` action to see all available test cases and their current status.
- Use the `tests_manager` tool with the `get` action to retrieve the full details of an untested test case.
- Due to context length and round limit, you should prioritize test cases that are more relevant to the identified issues.

### Step 2: Understand the Test Case
- Analyze the selected test case to understand its structure, input characteristics, and what it is testing.
- Call the `verify` tool to see how the LLVM IR code in the test case is transformed by the optimization pass. This can help you understand the semantics of the test case and how it relates to the fix.

### Step 3: Apply Mutations
For the selected test case, apply mutations according to your proposed test strategies:
- **Focus**: Ensure the mutations focus on exposing the potential issues identified in Phase 1.
- **Format**: Provide each mutated test case in a separate ```llvm ... ``` code block.
- **Coverage**: **CRITICAL**: You must cover all the proposed test strategies across different test cases to ensure comprehensive testing of potential issues.

### Step 4: Verify and Test
- **Verify Tool**: Use the `verify` tool to submit the generated test cases along with your reasoning. This uses `alive2` to check if the test cases expose any issues with the fix.
- **Difftest Tool**: Use the `difftest` tool to execute the original and optimized LLVM IR (transformed by `opt`) with specific input values to check for execution differences.

### Step 5: Analyze Results and Refine
- If the `verify` or `difftest` fails to find issues, refine the test case only if you believe unexplored potential issues remain. Otherwise, move on to other strategies or test cases. Check **Guidelines for Refining Test Cases** below for suggestions on how to refine test cases based on verification results.

### Step 6: Mark as Tested
- Once you have fully explored and verified a test case, use the `tests_manager` tool with the `mark_tested` action to mark it as completed.
- **CRITICAL**: You must repeat this process until the `tests_manager` confirms that **all** test cases have been tested.

Repeat the above steps for each test case until all test cases in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

You are also allowed to generate tests from scratch or find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools if you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues. Make sure you call `verify` and `difftest` tools to check the validity of these test cases and confirm whether the issues can be exposed by actual execution.

---

## Guidelines for Refining Test Cases

### Important Rule
You can always keep mutating the test case and verifying it until you find an issue or are confident that no issues can be found. However, make sure it is aligned with the potential issues you identified in Phase 1. Meanwhile, avoid stucking on a test case for too long. If you have tried multiple mutations and still cannot find any issues, move on to other test cases or strategies.

### Handling Verification Results
- **Failed-to-prove**:
  1. Try to reduce the test case to a smaller example that still fails. This can help isolate the specific conditions that cause the issue.
  2. Try to call `difftest` to run the test case with specific input value that you think can trigger the issue based on your analysis. This can help check if the issue can be exposed by actual execution even if it cannot be proved by alive2.
- **Alive2 errors**: Try to call `trans` to run opt with the same command arguments and adjust the test case or command arguments to fix.
- **Correct transformation**: Try to first analyze if the test strategy is correct and then decide to refine the test case or continue to generate other test cases.

### Additional Notes
- **Coverage**: If you think the provided test cases have limited coverage of potential issues, you can also find more related tests from the LLVM test suite by using `find` or `list` tools and reading them with `read` or `grep` tools.
- **Completeness**: Make sure you have at least explored all the test strategies you proposed in Phase 1, and generated multiple test cases if possible.
- **Completion**: You cannot finish Phase 2 until all tests in the `tests_manager` are marked as tested.

---

## Tools that you can use

- `findN`: Search for files in the LLVM codebase related to the component or optimization pass to understand the context of the fix and find relevant tests.
- `listN`: List files in the LLVM codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `readN`: Read the content of a file in the LLVM codebase to understand existing tests or the fix.
- `grepN`: Search for specific patterns in the codebase to find relevant tests or code regions.
- `tests_manager`: Manage the list of test cases. You can list all tests to see their status, get the details of a specific test, or mark a test as tested. Your goal is to ensure all tests are tested.
- `trans`: Run the `opt` tool with specific arguments to see how the LLVM IR code is transformed by the optimization pass.
- `verify`: Use alive2 to verify if the transformation from original LLVM IR code to optimized LLVM IR code is correct, which can help check the validity of generated test cases in Phase 2.
- `difftest`: Use llubi to perform differential testing on the original and transformed LLVM IR code, which can help check if the generated test cases cannot be proved by alive2.
- `report`: End Phase 2 by submitting the generated test cases and their verification results.

name: verify
arguments:
{"args":"-passes=slp-vectorizer","orig_ir":"```llvm
declare void @use4(i32, i32, i32, i32)

define void @test_copyable_mix(i32 %a, i32 %b, i32 %c, i32 %d, i32 %e, i32 %f, i32 %g, i32 %h) {
entry:
  ; main opcode will be add (commutative)
  %i0 = add i32 %a, %b
  ; different opcode, likely treated as copyable w.r.t add
  %i1 = urem i32 %c, %d
  %i2 = add i32 13, %e
  %i3 = add i32 %f, 7

  ; create 4 adds that mix copyable and non-copyable lanes, and flip order
  %x0 = add i32 %i0, %g
  %x1 = add i32 %h, %i1
  %x2 = add i32 %i2, %i3
  %x3 = add i32 %i1, 0

  call void @use4(i32 %x0, i32 %x1, i32 %x2, i32 %x3)
  ret void
}
```"}

arguments:
{"force":true,"test":["```llvm
; New regression-style patterns used to exercise SLP commuting logic.
; All are intended to be run with: opt -S -passes=slp-vectorizer -mtriple=x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu -slp-threshold=-99999
;
; 1) Per-lane commutation: alternating operand order in 4 scalar adds feeding contiguous stores.

define void @test_commute_store(ptr %p, i32 %a, i32 %b, i32 %c, i32 %d) {
entry:
  %x0 = add i32 %a, 1
  store i32 %x0, ptr %p, align 4
  %p1 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 1
  %x1 = add i32 2, %b
  store i32 %x1, ptr %p1, align 4
  %p2 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 2
  %x2 = add i32 %c, 3
  store i32 %x2, ptr %p2, align 4
  %p3 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 3
  %x3 = add i32 4, %d
  store i32 %x3, ptr %p3, align 4
  ret void
}

; 2) Almost-splat side: constants 5,5,5,7 with alternating operand order.

define i32 @test_almost_splat2(ptr %p, i32 %a, i32 %b, i32 %c, i32 %d) {
entry:
  %la = load i32, ptr %p, align 4
  %lb = add i32 %la, %a
  %lc = mul i32 %la, %b
  %ld = xor i32 %la, %c

  %x0 = add i32 %lb, 5
  %x1 = add i32 5, %lc
  %x2 = add i32 %ld, 5
  %x3 = add i32 7, %la

  store i32 %x0, ptr %p, align 4
  %p1 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 1
  store i32 %x1, ptr %p1, align 4
  %p2 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 2
  store i32 %x2, ptr %p2, align 4
  %p3 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 3
  store i32 %x3, ptr %p3, align 4

  ret i32 0
}

; 3) Copyable-element mix: urem lanes mixed into adds with alternating operand order.

define void @test_copyable_mix_store(ptr %p, i32 %a, i32 %b, i32 %c, i32 %d) {
entry:
  %i0 = add i32 %a, %b
  %i1 = urem i32 %c, %d

  %x0 = add i32 %i0, 1
  store i32 %x0, ptr %p, align 4
  %p1 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 1
  %x1 = add i32 2, %i1
  store i32 %x1, ptr %p1, align 4
  %p2 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 2
  %x2 = add i32 %i0, 3
  store i32 %x2, ptr %p2, align 4
  %p3 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 3
  %x3 = add i32 4, %i1
  store i32 %x3, ptr %p3, align 4

  ret void
}

; 4) Global-address/ConstantExpr-like operand: value loaded from internal global used in alternating-order adds.

@G = internal global [4 x i32] [i32 11, i32 22, i32 33, i32 44], align 16

define void @test_gep_global_store(ptr %p, i32 %a, i32 %b, i32 %c, i32 %d) {
entry:
  %ceptr = getelementptr [4 x i32], ptr @G, i64 0, i64 2
  %cev = load i32, ptr %ceptr, align 4

  %x0 = add i32 %a, %cev
  store i32 %x0, ptr %p, align 4
  %p1 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 1
  %x1 = add i32 %cev, %b
  store i32 %x1, ptr %p1, align 4
  %p2 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 2
  %x2 = add i32 %c, %cev
  store i32 %x2, ptr %p2, align 4
  %p3 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 3
  %x3 = add i32 %cev, %d
  store i32 %x3, ptr %p3, align 4

  ret void
}

; 5) Poison stress: poison generated by select; run with %c=true in execution testing.

define void @test_poison_store(i1 %c, ptr %p, i32 %a, i32 %b, i32 %d) {
entry:
  %pv = select i1 %c, i32 %a, i32 poison
  %x0 = add i32 %pv, 1
  store i32 %x0, ptr %p, align 4
  %p1 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 1
  %x1 = add i32 2, %b
  store i32 %x1, ptr %p1, align 4
  %p2 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 2
  %x2 = add i32 %d, 3
  store i32 %x2, ptr %p2, align 4
  %p3 = getelementptr i32, ptr %p, i64 3
  %x3 = add i32 4, %pv
  store i32 %x3, ptr %p3, align 4
  ret void
}
```","opt -S -passes=slp-vectorizer -mtriple=x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu -slp-threshold=-99999 < %s"],"thoughts":"Bug Triggering Analysis:
- These patterns are constructed so that SLP’s copyable-elements profitability logic is exercised for commutative ops with alternating operand order. In particular, the 4-store forms reliably trigger SLP vectorization, producing vector packs and a vector add feeding a vector store. This is exactly where per-lane commuting between Operands[0][i] and Operands[1][i] matters.
- We confirmed (via trans) that SLP does vectorize `@test_commute_store` into an insertelement chain + vector add + vector store, which is the transformation potentially impacted by the patch.

Fix Weakness Analysis:
- The patch uses `for (auto [OpA, OpB] : zip(Operands.front(), Operands.back())) std::swap(OpA, OpB);` which is at risk of not mutating `Operands` if structured bindings capture by value. If that happened, these patterns would stop vectorizing or would take a different path. The store-based patterns are good canaries because they tend to deterministically vectorize when the analysis succeeds.
- Additional variants cover: almost-splat constants (sensitive to which operand list CheckOperand is applied to), copyable-element mixing (urem lanes), and global-address-like operands (constant-ish operands beyond plain integers).

Verification results:
- Alive2 verification succeeded for the generated IR fragments (no miscompiles found).
- Differential execution testing (llubi) succeeded for integer store-based tests; no behavioral differences were found.
- llubi crashed for ptrtoint-based tests (interpreter limitation), so the global-address strategy was exercised with internal-global GEP+load instead, which difftested successfully.

Conclusion:
- No correctness bug found in the patch with these targeted tests; the tests mainly serve as regression coverage to ensure per-lane commuting continues to enable vectorization and remains semantics-preserving."}
